strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
2
In this topic, you'll learn the basics of biochemistry, the science of chemical processes in
organisms. This topic should give you the basic background information to understand
how the body produces energy, which it needs for everything from kidney functioning to
muscle contractions. As long as you understand the practical take-home messages,
you're fine as a Personal Trainer. All of this will be covered again in the next modules
on the 3 major macronutrients.

All matter is composed of particles. One of the most fundamental classes of chemical
elements a nutritionist needs to be aware of is the macronutrient groups. Macronutrients
are components of food that organisms need to function (nutrients) and are consumed in
large (macro) quantities. There are 3 important dietary macronutrients:
• Carbohydrates
• Protein
• Fat

There is also a fourth macronutrient that may be important for some of you - alcohol -
but we won't go into that here.

One thing that all 3 macros have in common is that their molecular structures are based
on a carbon skeleton, several carbons that are interconnected to form a carbon chain.

3
Carbohydrates
As the name suggests, carbohydrates are hydrated carbons; carbons (C) with water
(H20) attached to them. The most basic form of carbohydrate is glucose.

The molecular structure of glucose

Carbon is black yet sugar is white. To realize that white sugar consists of black carbon,
see what happens when you dehydrate sugar: watch this video.

4
Protein
A protein molecule is a long chain of amino acids (= polypeptide) that folds to give a 3-
dimensional structure. Amino acid molecules are connected to each other via peptide
bonds. Each amino acid consists of a carbon chain (α-carbon and the side chain) that
carries an amino group and an acid group. (The exception to this is the amino acid
glycine, which only contains the α-carbon and no side chain.)

5
Fat
Fatty acids consist of long carbon chains with an acid group (carboxyl group).

6
Energy production and fat storage
Knowing that all the 3 macronutrients are built from carbon chains is crucial to
understand the main principles of human metabolism.
1. The human body oxidizes ('burns') carbon chains to gain energy. For this reason,
we can gain energy from carbohydrates, protein and fat.
2. The breakdown products (metabolites) of carbohydrates, protein and fat can be
transferred into each other. This connects metabolic pathways and allows the
body to transform one nutrient class into another, such as storing carbohydrates
as fat in an energy surplus or making glucose out of the carbon skeletons of
amino acids when no glucose is ingested.

Below is a schematic diagram of a connection between different metabolic pathways,
showing how the body can create energy (directly or indirectly) from all 3
macronutrients.

7

An overview of how the body produces energy, fat and carbs.

As you can see, acetyl-CoA is the central metabolite for energy production (via
oxidation to carbon dioxide, CO2) and the body can obtain this from whichever
macronutrient you eat.
• From carbs, via intermediate conversion of glucose to pyruvate, a process called
glycolysis.
• From fat, specifically fatty acids.
• From protein, specifically amino acids.

Acetyl-CoA can also be converted to fat. Since the body can create acetyl-CoA from not
just fat but also indirectly from carbs and protein, the body can use all 3 major
macronutrients as substrate ('building blocks') to store body fat. All energy we consume
can make us fat.

8
ATP
The story of energy production in the human body essentially always ends with ATP
(adenosine triphosphate). ATP transports chemical energy that is required for metabolic
processes. ATP is basically a battery. Energy is released when ATP is hydrolyzed to
ADP (adenosine diphosphate), so ADP is basically a partially charged battery. ADP can
be further hydrolyzed to AMP (adenosine monophosphate) to release more energy and
fully deplete the battery.

9

Now that we understand the basics of energy production, let's zoom in on certain
metabolic pathways. These metabolic pathways explain how the body produces energy
from the food we eat and how it discards the waste products that are formed along the
way, such as carbon dioxide (CO2) and urea.

Below is a more detailed overview of human energy production. We'll start with the top
left green area.

10

Zooming in on how the body produces energy. Note the position of acetyl-CoA and
oxaloacetate in this figure and compare them to the previous zoomed-out overview.
You're not expected to understand this graphic yet, but after the modules on each
macronutrient, come back to this overview and you should fully understand it.

11
Energy production from carbohydrates
As we'll explain in more detail in the specific module on carbs, most carbs we eat are
broken down to glucose. Each glucose molecule is then broken down into smaller
molecules in a process called glycolysis. The product of glycolysis is pyruvate.
Glycolysis already releases some energy (ATP), but pyruvate can be broken down
further in 2 ways.

First, as you learned, pyruvate be broken down to acetyl-CoA, which can be stored as
body fat for future energy production or used for immediate energy production. Energy
production from acetyl-CoA occurs via the so-called TCA cycle and oxidative
phosphorylation. All acetyl-CoA is completely oxidized, burned up, leaving only CO2,
which we expel through our lungs, and water, H2O. As the name suggests, this type of
energy production occurs in aerobic (oxygen containing) conditions.

When oxygen is not available, in anaerobic conditions, or when the demand for rapid
energy is high, pyruvate is converted to lactate. The conversion to lactate occurs mainly
in times of strenuous exercise when the demand for oxygen by the working muscles
exceeds what is available.

12
The tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle
The tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, also called the Krebs cycle or the citric acid cycle, is
a vital energy production pathway for the body. Metabolites of carbohydrate, fat and
protein that enter the cycle can be completely oxidized to CO2 and H2O with an
accompanying release of energy (though some intermediate substrates are formed that
are used to i.a. 'recycle' back into glucose or amino acids).

You don't need to understand the specifics of the TCA cycle, but for reference, in a
process called decarboxylation, CO2 is removed from pyruvate, the product of
glycolysis, to form acetyl-CoA. The product of pyruvate is acetyl, which is coupled to
Coenzyme A (CoA) that functions as a carrier. Acetyl-CoA is incorporated into
oxaloacetic acid to form citric acid and subsequently lost as CO2 in the decarboxylation
step of the TCA cycle. Two important products of the TCA cycle are NADH and FADH2.
These products can be used to reduce O2 to H2O in a process that is called oxidative
phosphorylation, during which ATP is formed.

Another product of the TCA cycle is GTP, which has a similar function to ATP but
possesses higher 'reaction specificity': it can only be used for specific reactions.

All you need to understand is that the TCA cycle and subsequent oxidative
phosphorylation are essentially a metabolic furnace. The body throws in the metabolites
of the macronutrients we eat and they get burned in the furnace to give us energy.

13
Further reading
If you are interested in the nitty gritty of biochemistry, the book Advanced Nutrition and
Human Metabolism is a great reference. It is difficult to read, however, and contains far
more information than you need to know as a nutritionist, so all the important topics
from that book are summarized in a more reader friendly format in the course
documents.

14
Learning objectives
This module should teach you basic biochem terminology and how the body produces
energy. All of this will be detailed further and repeated in the sections where it becomes
relevant for practical application, but you should understand some basic biochemistry
and terminology by now, including the following.
• ATP
• Aerobic vs. anaerobic metabolism
• Glycolysis
• Metabolites
• Oxidation

➢ Click here to try example exam questions for this
module.
